votes.reason: The Python code correctly computes the expected largest negative and smallest positive integers (or None if they don't exist) and compares them to the given inputs, matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly computes the largest negative and smallest positive integers (or None if they don't exist) and compares them to the given inputs, matching the Coq specification.

The Coq spec defines a as None when no negatives exist, or Some m where m is the largest negative (max of negatives). Similarly, b is None when no positives exist, or Some p where p is the smallest positive (min of positives). The Python code computes exactly these values: max of negatives for a, min of positives for b, with None when the respective lists are empty. The concrete behavior matches.

The Coq spec says a is None iff there are no negatives, otherwise a is the largest negative (max of negatives). Similarly, b is None iff there are no positives, otherwise b is the smallest positive (min of positives). The Python code computes exactly this: expected_a = None if no negatives else max(negatives), expected_b = None if no positives else min(positives), and returns whether a and b match these expected values.

The Coq spec defines a as None when no negatives exist, otherwise the largest negative (max of negatives satisfying n <= m for all negatives n). Similarly, b is None when no positives exist, otherwise the smallest positive (min of positives satisfying p <= n for all positives n). The Python code computes exactly this: expected_a = None if no negatives else max(negatives), and expected_b = None if no positives else min(positives). Both return the same concrete results for any input list and candidate values a, b.

Both require a be the maximum negative (or None if none) and b be the minimum positive (or None if none). The Python code computes exactly these and compares, using None/int to represent Coq option values.
